title: A Feast for the Seaweeds
description: A controversial Arabic novel about exile, disillusionment, and radical politics.
descriptionParagraphs: A controversial Arabic novel about exile, disillusionment, and radical politics.

A Feast for the Seaweeds is usually read through its treatment of religion, politics, and exile. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes religion, politics, and exile feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: A controversial Arabic novel about exile, disillusionment, and radical politics.
banningParagraphs: A Feast for the Seaweeds entered censorship debates as a novel associated with religion, politics, and exile. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around blasphemy and heresy.

The earliest event currently captured here is 2000-2001 in Egypt, where Al-Azhar-backed authorities and Egyptian officials confiscated and banned. Clerical protests turned the novel into a national controversy and triggered confiscation. The case shows how quickly republication can reactivate old censorship lines.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
